did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a customized Visual Geometry Group (VGG16) Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model with transfer learning to detect and classify melanoma from dermoscopic images. The methodology includes preprocessing, feature extraction via CNN layers, and binary classification using a sigmoid output layer, evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a customized VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network for melanoma detection. It details specific methodology including data preparation using the HAM10000 dataset, transfer learning strategies, training processes, and performance evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score). Although published in conference proceedings, it reports new results from a technical study rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Detection of Melanoma from Dermoscopic Images using Visual Geometry Group (VGG16) Model based Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
journal: Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Inventive Computing and Informatics (ICICI-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated melanoma detection system using a customized Visual Geometry Group 16 (VGG16) Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset, utilizing transfer learning from ImageNet weights and data augmentation techniques to handle class imbalance and improve generalization. Preprocessing steps included resizing images to 224x224 pixels and removing artifacts such as hair and noise. The system was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics, with Grad-CAM employed for model interpretability. Experimental results demonstrated that the customized VGG16 architecture achieved improved F1 scores, recall, and precision compared to the conventional VGG16 model in distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the binary classification task, defined as malignant cases in the HAM10000 dataset.
The negative class in the binary classification task, representing non-melanoma cases in the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign or malignant (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'detect dermoscopic images as benign or malign through precise classification'. The model uses a sigmoid output layer for binary classification (melanoma as 1 and benign as 0) and evaluates performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The target is discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, with comparisons made based on these metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: Results for the testing dataset are presented in Figure 8. The testing set consisted of 1000 images used to evaluate the final model performance.

ISIC Dataset.notes: Figure 8 labels the x-axis categories as 'ISIC Dataset' and 'PH2 Dataset' alongside the metrics, but the text clarifies the testing set was 1000 images. The specific metric values 0.99, 0.98, and 0.97 are associated with the 'Testing data' bar chart.

PH2 Dataset.notes: Figure 8 labels the x-axis categories as 'ISIC Dataset' and 'PH2 Dataset' alongside the metrics. The paper mentions PH2 as a data source but does not provide separate quantitative results for PH2 distinct from the aggregate training/testing results.

architecture_used.main_model: Customized Visual Geometry Group 16 (VGG16) Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)

architecture_details.description: The customized architecture includes blocks of max-pooling layers and convolutional layers with small receptive fields (3x3). It differs from standard VGG16 by specific layer configurations and the addition of a dropout layer.

training_details.normalization: Pixel values normalized to range (0,1) by dividing by 255

classification_or_output_head.description: Sigmoid activation function used for binary classification (melanoma as 1, benign as 0). Output is a probability value.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224x224 pixels to comply with VGG16 input size requirements
Normalization of pixel values to the range (0,1) by dividing by 255
Removal of artifacts such as hair and noise
Lesion detection/separation from surrounding healthy tissue using a binary mask
Data augmentation techniques including random rotation, flipping, scaling, and brightness adjustment

conclusions: The customized VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network architecture demonstrated improved efficacy compared to the conventional VGG16 model, achieving balanced classification of benign and malignant cases.
Experimental results showed that the customized architecture achieved improved F1 scores, recall, and precision in identifying benign and malignant melanoma cases.
The study achieved high performance metrics on the testing dataset, with an accuracy of 0.99, precision of 0.98, and F1-score of 0.97.

The methodology of preprocessing dermoscopic images to enhance quality and eliminate artifacts like hair and noise, combined with transfer learning and data augmentation, effectively enhanced generalization and performance.
The use of Grad-CAM for explainability provided transparency by displaying regions in the image contributing most to the model's prediction, relating to clinical features employed by dermatologists.
The automated system has the potential to reshape early detection and treatment of melanoma, improving patient outcomes and healthcare delivery.
Ongoing research is needed to advance model interpretability and clinical integration in real-time settings.
important_remarks: The paper reports exceptionally high performance metrics (Accuracy: 0.99, Precision: 0.98, F1-Score: 0.97) on the testing set, which may indicate overfitting or data leakage given the small testing size (1000 images) and lack of external validation.
The study lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of the performance improvements over the conventional VGG16 model.
The dataset split is heavily imbalanced in favor of training (6000 images) compared to validation and testing (1000 images each), potentially limiting the robustness of the evaluation.

The paper mentions using the ISIC and PH2 datasets as sources but does not provide distinct quantitative results for PH2, making it unclear if the model was truly validated on external datasets or if the 'ISIC Dataset' label in results refers to the HAM10000 subset.

The model was trained for only 10 epochs, which is a very short duration for deep learning tasks, raising concerns about whether the model fully converged or if early stopping criteria were strictly defined.

The preprocessing step involves removing artifacts like hair and noise, which may introduce bias if the algorithm's ability to handle real-world noisy images (where such preprocessing might fail or alter features) is not tested.

The authors are from a single institution in India, and the dataset (HAM10000) has known demographic biases; the paper does not discuss the generalizability of the model across different skin types or geographic populations.
The paper claims 'privacy-preserving, real-time analysis' in the system design section but provides no evidence, latency metrics, or deployment architecture to support these claims.
